📸 Real. Raw. Urgent.

They Are Suffering Right Now — On Your Street

These are not distant images. These are the dogs and cows outside your home, your office, your colony — starving, injured, and dying of thirst every summer while we look away.

🐕 Street Dogs — Starving, Thirsty & Injured
Shelter under any shadeThese Indian stray dogs squeeze under parked cars to escape 45°C heat — no water, no food, no one to help them.
Collapsed from heatStreet dogs collapse on burning pavements with no shade, no water, no one to help — in 45°C summer heat.
Injured & untreatedHit by vehicles, attacked, or ill — injured stray dogs lie on roadsides. Without care, wounds become fatal in days.
Drinking dirty water to surviveWith no clean water available, dogs drink from sewage puddles. Disease and death follow — one bowl outside your door changes this.
Mange, illness, sufferingSkin diseases, mange, and infections spread rapidly in summer heat. Most stray dogs never receive any medical attention.
Born on the streetStray puppies are born with nothing — no shelter, no mother's milk, no food. Many don't survive their first summer.
🐦 Sparrows & Birds — Dying of Thirst in Summer
A sparrow finds waterA small clay pot of water outside your window can save dozens of sparrows, bulbuls, and parrots every single day of summer.
Relief at lastBirds not only drink — they bathe to cool down in summer heat. A shallow clay dish is enough to save them.
India's disappearing sparrowHouse sparrow populations have fallen over 60% in Indian cities. Heat, thirst, and habitat loss are killing them.
🐄 Indian Stray Cows — Abandoned, Starving & Eating Garbage
Desperate for any waterThese thin white Indian cows crowd around an almost-dry stone trough. Their ribs are visible. This is what daily survival looks like in summer.
Wandering without purposeA herd of Indian cows roams a dusty road with nowhere to go — no pasture, no water trough, no owner to care for them.
Every bone visibleEvery rib, every hip bone exposed. This cow is in the final stages of starvation — a daily reality on Indian streets that we ignore.
Too weak to standThis cow is so malnourished it can no longer stand. Collapsed on bare earth — no grass, no water, no care nearby.
Young India taking actionYouth across India are stepping up — feeding cows, carrying water, doing what adults and governments haven't. Be one of them.
The greatness of a nation can be judged by the way its animals are treated. — Mahatma Gandhi
⚠️ Warning Signs

How to Tell if an Animal is in Distress

🫁

Excessive Panting

Rapid, heavy breathing with mouth wide open is one of the earliest and most visible signs of dangerous overheating in dogs and cats.

🥴

Lethargy & Collapse

An animal that is too weak to stand, stumbling, or lying flat without responding to sound or touch is in immediate danger.

👅

Dry, Pale Gums

Healthy gums are pink and moist. Pale, dry, or sticky gums signal severe dehydration — a life-threatening emergency.

👁️

Sunken Eyes

Eyes that appear deeply sunken and dull rather than bright and round indicate the animal has been without water for a dangerous period.

🩺

Rapid Heartbeat

A racing pulse combined with hot dry skin means the body is struggling to regulate temperature and is under extreme stress.

🦴

Visible Ribs & Bones

If you can clearly see the outline of ribs and spine, the animal is severely malnourished and needs food and vet care urgently.

☀️ The Danger

What Summer Does to Unprotected Animals

  • 🌡️

    Heatstroke

    When body temperature exceeds 41°C, organs begin to fail. Strays with no shade or water can die within an hour on a peak summer day.

  • 💧

    Severe Dehydration

    Animals lose water fast through panting and sweating paws. Without a water source, dehydration becomes fatal in hours on hot days.

  • 🥩

    Starvation

    Stray animals rely on scraps and garbage. Summer heat drives people indoors, cutting off food sources, leaving animals starving on scorching streets.

  • 🔥

    Burned Paw Pads

    Asphalt can reach 70°C+ in full sun. Walking on it burns and blisters paws, often leading to infections and inability to move to find food.

  • 🦟

    Fly Strike & Infection

    Open wounds attract flies in summer. Fly larvae can infest skin quickly, causing life-threatening myiasis if untreated within 24 hours.

🌡️ Temperature Danger Zones

Safe for dogs
< 25°C
Caution zone
25–32°C
Danger zone
32–40°C
Life-threatening
40°C +

A parked car in summer sun can reach 70°C in minutes — never leave an animal inside.

💚 Take Action

Simple Ways You Can Help Right Now

01 💧

Place Water Bowls Outside

The single most powerful thing you can do. A clay or steel bowl in shade outside your home or shop can save dozens of lives daily.

  • Refill twice a day — morning and evening
  • Use wide, low bowls so small animals can drink too
  • Keep in shade to prevent quick evaporation
  • Clean the bowl every few days
02 🍚

Feed Stray Animals

Plain cooked rice with a little dal or curd is safe and nutritious for stray dogs and cats. Even leftover bread or biscuits help.

  • Feed in early morning or after sunset (cooler)
  • Avoid spicy, oily, or heavily salted food
  • Cooked rice, oats, eggs are safe for dogs
  • Keep a small bag of biscuits in your bag
03 🏕️

Create Shade Spots

A piece of cardboard, a jute sack, or a small tarp propped against a wall gives animals a shaded refuge from direct sun.

  • Old cardboard boxes make quick shelters
  • Alert your housing society or RWA
  • Trees and bushes are natural shade — don't cut them
  • Damp cloth on ground cools paws instantly
04 🩺

Rescue & Report

If you see an animal in critical distress — unconscious, severely injured, or unable to move — act immediately.

  • Move animal to shade gently using a cloth
  • Offer water slowly — don't force-pour
  • Wet their paws, belly, and neck with cool water
  • Call local NGO, SPCA, or animal helpline
📋 Quick Reference

Do's & Don'ts When Helping Animals

✔ Do These

  • Offer cool (not ice-cold) water slowly to a distressed animal
  • Move the animal to shade or an air-conditioned space if possible
  • Wet paws, belly, and neck with a damp cloth to lower temperature
  • Call an animal NGO or vet immediately for severely ill animals
  • Put out a fresh water bowl daily in your neighbourhood
  • Speak to local shopkeepers and colonies to place water points
  • Report cruelly treated or trapped animals to local authorities
  • Carry a bottle of water on walks to share with street dogs

✘ Never Do These

  • Never pour water forcefully into a semiconscious animal's mouth
  • Never use ice or ice-cold water — it can cause shock
  • Never leave animals locked in vehicles in summer, even briefly
  • Never feed chocolate, onion, garlic, grapes, or raisins to dogs
  • Never chase or shout at a heat-stressed animal — it worsens panic
  • Never ignore an animal lying still in the sun — check on them
  • Never remove a rescued animal from vet care too early
  • Never use plastic bowls in extreme heat — they leach chemicals
💧 Water Stations

Set Up a Community Water Station

Organised water stations around your neighbourhood can save hundreds of animals every summer. Here's what works best:

🏺

Clay / Matka Bowl

Naturally keeps water cool for hours. Best choice for outdoor water stations in summer heat.

🥣

Steel Tray

Wide, low, heavy-duty. Great for birds and small animals. Easy to clean and refill daily.

🌿

Shaded Placement

Place under trees, against north-facing walls, or under awnings. Water stays cooler longer.

🏘️

Community Network

Ask 5 neighbours to each maintain one bowl. Together you create a life-saving water grid.

🛠️ DIY Water Station in 5 Minutes

  1. Find a large clay pot, steel tray, or wide plastic basin (avoid narrow bottles).
  2. Fill with clean, cool tap water — refill every morning and evening.
  3. Place in a shaded area at ground level where animals can easily access it.
  4. Put a small flat stone or piece of wood inside so insects and small creatures can escape.
  5. Write "Free Water for Animals 🐾" in your local language nearby to inspire others.
📍 Gurugram, Haryana

A Small Act of Kindness, Started in Gurugram

This is a small, heartfelt initiative started by Gaurav — a regular person from Gurugram who decided that watching animals suffer in scorching summer heat was no longer something he could ignore.

Every morning, he places water bowls outside his home and feeds stray dogs in his neighbourhood. No organisation. No funding. Just one person, one city, and the belief that every life matters — whether it has two legs or four.

With a little support from kind people, the goal is to scale this — more water stations, more regular feeding, basic vet care for injured strays.

🙏 If this touched your heart — please do something similar in your city. Place one bowl of water. Feed one stray. You don't need to start big. Just start.
🐕

What Your Support Funds

  • 💧 Clay water pots placed across neighbourhoods
  • 🍚 Daily food for stray dogs and cats
  • 🩺 Emergency vet care for injured strays
  • 🌿 Shaded rest spots using simple materials
  • 📢 Spreading awareness in local communities
Even ₹50 can provide clean water and food to a stray animal for an entire week. 🐾
